papers.title: Exercise for multidirectional instability of the shoulder.
papers.journal: The Cochrane database of systematic reviews
papers.abstract: Multidirectional shoulder instability is characterised by symptomatic subluxation or dislocation in at least two directions, often affecting young, active individuals. Although exercise therapy is commonly recommended as a first-line treatment, its benefits and harms remain uncertain.
papers.methods: No specific study design or sample size described, this abstract provides background information and highlights a research gap.
findings: Multidirectional shoulder instability is characterized by symptomatic subluxation or dislocation in at least two directions.
This condition frequently affects young, active individuals.
Despite exercise therapy being a commonly recommended first-line treatment, its benefits and harms for multidirectional shoulder instability remain uncertain.
papers.implications: When treating young, active individuals with multidirectional shoulder instability, physiotherapists should acknowledge the current uncertainty regarding the benefits and harms of exercise therapy, ensuring clear communication with patients and meticulous monitoring of individual treatment responses.

papers.title: Rehabilitation efficacy of Low-Load blood flow restriction training for lateral patellar compression syndrome.
papers.journal: Journal of orthopaedic surgery and research
papers.abstract: Arthroscopic lateral retinacular release (LRR) is an effective treatment for lateral patellar compression syndrome (LPCS), but postoperative rehabilitation remains crucial. Low-load blood flow restriction training (LL-BFRT) has been shown to be beneficial in sports medicine; however, its effect on postoperative recovery in LPCS is unclear.
papers.methods: Not applicable; this abstract identifies a research gap rather than presenting a specific study design or sample size.
findings: Arthroscopic lateral retinacular release (LRR) is recognized as an effective treatment for lateral patellar compression syndrome (LPCS).
Postoperative rehabilitation is crucial for optimal recovery following LRR for LPCS.
Low-load blood flow restriction training (LL-BFRT) has demonstrated general benefits in sports medicine, but its specific effect on postoperative recovery in LPCS is currently unclear.
papers.implications: For patients undergoing LRR for LPCS, continue to emphasize comprehensive postoperative rehabilitation. While LL-BFRT shows promise in general sports medicine, its specific application and efficacy for LPCS recovery still require dedicated research before routine integration into clinical practice.

papers.title: Effectiveness of repeated ultrasound-guided suprascapular nerve blocks in hemiplegic shoulder pain: A randomized controlled trial.
papers.abstract: Hemiplegic shoulder pain (HSP) is a common complication after stroke, significantly impairing rehabilitation. While suprascapular nerve block (SSNB) has shown promise in pain management, its long-term efficacy, especially with repeated applications, remains unclear.
papers.methods: Not applicable; this abstract describes a clinical problem and a knowledge gap regarding current treatment efficacy, rather than presenting a specific study design or sample.
findings: Hemiplegic shoulder pain (HSP) is a common complication after stroke.
HSP significantly impairs the rehabilitation process.
The long-term efficacy of suprascapular nerve block (SSNB), especially with repeated applications for pain management, is currently unclear.
papers.implications: For physiotherapists managing HSP, while suprascapular nerve block (SSNB) may be considered for short-term pain relief given its promise, it is essential to focus on integrating it within a comprehensive, evidence-based rehabilitation program, as the sustained benefits and safety of repeated applications are not yet established.

papers.title: Postoperative weight-bearing restrictions and rehabilitation after periacetabular osteotomy: a systematic review.
papers.journal: Journal of orthopaedic surgery and research
papers.abstract: This systematic review aimed to synthesize current evidence on postoperative rehabilitation strategies, particularly weight-bearing restrictions after periacetabular osteotomy (PAO).
findings: This abstract primarily outlines the study's aim to synthesize current evidence on postoperative rehabilitation strategies.
A specific focus of the systematic review is on weight-bearing restrictions following periacetabular osteotomy (PAO).
papers.implications: Physiotherapists should anticipate and monitor the full findings of systematic reviews on PAO rehabilitation to guide evidence-based decisions regarding appropriate postoperative weight-bearing protocols.

papers.title: Female Representation in Rock Climbing Research.
papers.abstract: IntroductionAlthough rock climbing is approaching gender parity in participation, female representation in rock climbing research remains to be investigated. We evaluated the gender distribution of study participants and authorship in the rock climbing literature.MethodsWe conducted a systematic review with PubMed and Embase to identify original research articles involving rock climbers. We categorized articles by and extracted data on the sex (female and male) of study participants and authors. Author gender was classified with Genderize.io, an automated probability-based assessment software.ResultsWe identified 298 original research articles from 1973 to 2023. Among the 266 articles that reported study participant gender, females comprised 36% of climber participants. Eighty-two articles (31%) had all-male climber participants, whereas 7 articles (3%) had an all-female cohort. Female climbers were the most underrepresented in articles investigating chronic climbing injuries (13% female), imaging studies (15%), and injury rehabilitation (20%). Female representation was highest in studies about youth climbing (43%) and epidemiology (36%). Of 1360 total authorships, 290 (21%) were female. Females were first authors for 54 articles (18%) and last authors for 53 articles (18%). Articles with female first authors had a higher proportion of female study participants than those with male first authors (39 vs 36%; P<0.001), whereas the proportion was similar between female and male last authors (36 vs 36%; P=0.592).ConclusionsFemale representation in climbing research and authorship is limited. Most research has studied male climbers, limiting the ability to apply these results to female climbers.
papers.methods: Systematic Review, n=298 original research articles.
papers.stats: P<0.001 (articles with female first authors had a higher proportion of female study participants); P=0.592 (proportion of female study participants similar between female and male last authors).
findings: Females comprised 36% of climber participants across 266 articles that reported gender, with 31% of articles having all-male participants and 3% all-female.
Female climbers were most underrepresented in chronic climbing injuries (13% female), imaging studies (15%), and injury rehabilitation (20%).

Female representation among authors was 21% of 1360 total authorships, with females as first or last authors for 18% of articles. Articles with female first authors had a higher proportion of female study participants (39% vs 36%, P<0.001).
papers.implications: Physiotherapists should be critical of research findings in rock climbing, particularly concerning injury mechanisms, prevention, and rehabilitation, as most evidence is derived from male populations. Tailor assessments and interventions for female climbers based on individual presentation and specific female physiological considerations, rather than solely relying on generalized research findings.

papers.title: Morphological Changes and MRI Characteristics of the Achilles Tendon in Amateur Marathon Runners With Different Running Experience.
papers.journal: Journal of foot and ankle research
papers.abstract: With the escalating popularity of marathon running, Achilles tendon injuries, particularly gradual-onset Achilles tendon injury, have become common, often causing substantial training disruptions. However, the influence of running experience on the Achilles tendon structure in amateur runners remains largely unclear. This study aimed to investigate the association between running experience and asymptomatic Achilles tendon pathology as well as its structural changes.
papers.methods: Study design and sample size not explicitly stated in this abstract.
findings: This abstract outlines the background and aim of the study, but does not present any findings or results.
papers.implications: Clinical implications cannot be drawn from this abstract, as it only describes the study's aim and background, not its results.

papers.title: Female trail running: a systematic scoping review.
papers.journal: Frontiers in sports and active living
papers.abstract: Trail running's popularity among women is increasing, yet research addressing the unique physiological demands, performance factors, and injury patterns in this population remains limited. This scoping systematic review aimed to map the existing research landscape on female trail runners; synthesize current evidence across physiological, nutritional, injury, and performance domains; and identify critical knowledge gaps to guide future investigations. A systematic search was conducted across four major databases (EBSCO, PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science) up to December 2024. Eligible studies were original peer-reviewed articles reporting sex-specific data on female trail runners within the above domains. Methodological quality was assessed using the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) checklist. Results from the 22 included studies (pooled sample ≈ 2,476 participants), predominantly published in the last decade, primarily focused on physiology and biomarkers (10 studies) and nutrition and body composition (6 studies), with fewer investigations into injuries (4 studies) or performance (3 studies). These studies indicated significant exercise-induced physiological stress and highlighted links between nutrition, body composition, and performance outcomes. However, a recurring limitation was the pervasive inconsistency in reporting participant characteristics, especially evident for key female-specific factors such as menstrual cycle status and hormonal contraceptive use, which were sparsely detailed. Furthermore, considerable heterogeneity in methodologies and the poor reporting of race characteristics and environmental conditions limited the synthesis of actionable insights. Most included studies (21 out of 22) demonstrated good methodological quality. In conclusion, while the research on female trail runners is growing, its practical application and the ability to draw robust conclusions are constrained by widespread reporting inconsistencies and a notable lack of depth in female-specific physiological data. Further progress in this field relies on the adoption of standardized reporting guidelines and a concerted effort to conduct robust, longitudinal investigations. Future studies should address hormonal influences, energy availability, effective training methodologies, and targeted injury prevention strategies tailored to female trail runners, ultimately to optimize their health, well-being, and athletic potential.
papers.methods: Scoping Systematic Review, n=2,476 (pooled sample from 22 studies)
findings: 22 studies (pooled sample ≈ 2,476 participants), predominantly published in the last decade, were included.
Research primarily focused on physiology and biomarkers (10 studies) and nutrition/body composition (6 studies), with fewer investigations into injuries (4 studies) or performance (3 studies).
A significant limitation was the pervasive inconsistency in reporting female-specific factors such as menstrual cycle status and hormonal contraceptive use across the included studies.
papers.implications: When assessing female trail runners, physiotherapists should conduct a comprehensive intake to detail menstrual cycle status, hormonal contraceptive use, and nutritional habits, as these factors are often overlooked in current research but are critical for understanding individual physiological responses and injury risk.

papers.title: Occlusion pressure and blood pressure adaptations following low-load blood flow restriction training versus moderate-load training: a pilot randomized clinical trial in patients with knee osteoarthritis.
papers.abstract: This pilot study investigated potential adaptations in limb occlusion pressure (LOP) and compared LOP between low-load resistance training with blood flow restriction (LL-BFR) and traditional moderate-load training (ML) over 9 weeks in patients with knee osteoarthritis (KOA). Secondarily, we compared systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) adaptations between these groups.
findings: Limb occlusion pressure (LOP) adaptations were comparable between LL-BFR and ML groups after 9 weeks.
No significant differences were found in resting systolic or diastolic blood pressure adaptations between protocols.
papers.implications: LL-BFR can be safely prescribed for KOA patients to achieve similar vascular adaptations as moderate-load training without adverse effects on systemic blood pressure.
papers.stats: 9-week protocol; non-significant difference in LOP and BP between groups.

papers.title: Improvement of sarcopenia after total knee arthroplasty in patients with knee osteoarthritis.
papers.abstract: PurposeThis study aimed to investigate changes in sarcopenia status and physical function after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) in patients with knee osteoarthritis (KOA). MethodsThis prospective observational study was conducted at a single general hospital. Seventy-one patients with KOA undergoing first TKA were classified into sarcopenia (n = 12) and non-sarcopenia (n = 59) groups based on the 2019 Asian Working Group for Sarcopenia criteria. Outcomes including walking speed, grip strength, skeletal muscle mass index (SMI), Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS), knee joint muscle strength, and performance-based tests were evaluated preoperatively and at 6 and 12 months postoperatively. Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) with age and sex as covariates was used to examine differences in improvement at 6 and 12 months. Clinical equivalence was evaluated using 90% confidence intervals and minimum clinically important differences. ResultsOf the 12 patients with preoperative sarcopenia, 10 (83.3%) improved at 12 months postoperatively. ANCOVA showed significant adjusted mean difference (aMD) of 0.31 for SMI at 12 months. No significant differences were found in grip strength (aMD: 0.60), KOOS pain (aMD: 0.01), or 40-m fast-paced walk test (aMD: -0.03). Clinical equivalence was observed for SMI, grip strength, KOOS pain, and 40-m fast-paced walk test, but not other outcomes. ConclusionsIn patients with KOA and sarcopenia, TKA combined with postoperative rehabilitation was associated with improvements in sarcopenia-related measures. Improvements in gait ability, grip strength, and appendicular skeletal muscle mass were comparable to those observed in non-sarcopenic patients.
findings: 83.3% (10 of 12) of patients with preoperative sarcopenia showed resolution 12 months post-TKA.
Significant improvement in skeletal muscle mass index (SMI) observed at 12 months (aMD: 0.31).
Improvements in gait and grip strength were clinically equivalent to non-sarcopenic peers.
papers.implications: TKA combined with rehabilitation is highly effective for reversing sarcopenia; clinicians should focus on muscle mass index as a primary indicator of recovery.

papers.title: Addressing Shoulder Weakness in Individuals With Rotator Cuff-Related Shoulder Pain: A Systematic Review With Meta-analysis.
papers.journal: The Journal of orthopaedic and sports physical therapy
papers.abstract: Twenty-eight studies were included in the meta-analysis. Rehabilitation interventions had a moderate effect on shoulder external rotation strength (standardized mean difference [SMD], 0.56; 95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.39, 0.74; I2 = 78%; P < .01) and a small effect on shoulder abduction (SMD, 0.47; 95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.32, 0.63; I2 = 54%; P < .01), internal rotation (SMD, 0.41; 95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.30, 0.52; I2 = 25%; P < .08), flexion (SMD, 0.25; 95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.01, 0.49; I2 = 0%; P = .46), and scaption (SMD, 0.48; 95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.27, 0.70; I2 = 35%; P = .14) strength. Interventions that included strength training or active exercises had small-to-moderate effects on all strength outcomes. Interventions without exercises or strength training had no effects.
findings: Rehab had the strongest impact on external rotation (SMD 0.56) and abduction (SMD 0.47) strength.
Interventions lacking active exercise or strength training resulted in zero strength gains.
papers.implications: Prioritize active resistance training over passive modalities, specifically targeting external rotation to maximize strength recovery in RCRSP.

papers.title: Manual massage versus foam rolling within the NASM corrective framework: a trial for upper crossed syndrome rehabilitation in university students.
papers.abstract: This quasi-experimental, two-arm parallel-group trial evaluated the differential impact of manual massage versus foam rolling within a 12-week NASM-based corrective exercise protocol among 30 male university students (aged 18-25) with diagnosed UCS. MMG showed significantly greater improvements in RSA (p = 0.031), pain reduction (p = 0.003), shoulder external and internal ROM (p = 0.001 and p = 0.016), and SF-36 physical and mental component scores (p = 0.001).
findings: Manual massage was significantly more effective than foam rolling for reducing Rounded Shoulder Angle (p=0.031).
Massage group experienced superior pain reduction (p=0.003) and shoulder ROM gains.
papers.implications: Manual myofascial release should be prioritized over self-foam rolling when deeper tissue inhibition is needed for Upper Crossed Syndrome.

papers.title: Impact of multimodal education management on postoperative rehabilitation after total knee arthroplasty: A machine learning-based prediction model study.
papers.abstract: A total of 223 patients who underwent total knee arthroplasty from October 2022 to January 2025 were included. Multimodal education management using IPVE was significantly associated with better rehabilitation quality. Multivariate logistic regression showed that each 1-year increase in age reduced the probability of high-quality rehabilitation by 17.2% (P<.001), each 1Â° increase in knee range of motion at discharge increased the probability by 17.3% (P<.001).
findings: Video-based illustrated pathways (IPVE) significantly improved rehabilitation quality.
Knee ROM at discharge is a critical predictor; every 1 degree extra increases high-quality recovery odds by 17.3%.
papers.implications: Incorporate video-based education early to improve patient adherence and discharge range of motion.
papers.stats: ROM OR: 1.173 (17.3% gain per degree); Age OR: 0.828; p < 0.001.

papers.title: Running into deficits: Post-operative bracing limits knee extension recovery 6 months after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction.
papers.abstract: To evaluate whether postoperative knee bracing impacts functional recovery following anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction (ACLR) using hamstring autografts.
papers.methods: Kinematic Joint Analysis, n not specified
findings: Postoperative bracing significantly limited the recovery of full knee extension 6 months post-surgery.
Restricted extension impacted kinematic patterns during gait, running, and jumping.
papers.implications: Minimize the use of restrictive bracing to allow for early return of full knee extension, which is vital for normal running mechanics.
papers.stats: Significant extension deficits at 6-month follow-up in braced cohorts.

papers.title: Effectiveness of an online multi-component exercise-based injury prevention program on running-related injury risk in recreational runners: a randomized controlled trial.
papers.abstract: This randomized controlled trial (n=264) assessed an online, multi-component exercise program (core, flexibility, neuromuscular control) performed 2-4 times weekly. The intervention group showed significantly lower rates of overall running-related injuries compared to controls.
findings: The intervention group had a 47% risk reduction in overall running-related injuries (IRR = 0.53).
Overuse injuries specifically were reduced by 54% (p = 0.001).
papers.implications: Prescribe a 15-30 minute online-guided routine focusing on core and neuromuscular control 2x/week to effectively halve injury risk in recreational runners.

papers.title: Runners with a high body mass index and previous running-related problems is a high-risk population for sustaining a new running-related injury: A 18-month cohort study.
papers.authors: European Journal of Sport Science Group
papers.abstract: Part of the 'Garmin-RUNSAFE' study, this cohort tracked runners to evaluate if elevated BMI combined with previous injury history created a 'vulnerable subgroup' for new injuries.
findings: Runners with BMI >30 kg/mÂ² AND a previous injury history faced a 71% injury risk.
Runners with normal BMI (19-25) and no history had only a 43% risk.
papers.implications: Screen for the 'BMI + History' combined phenotype; these patients require more conservative volume progression than those with just one risk factor.
papers.stats: 71% cumulative incidence risk in high-risk group vs 43% in low-risk.

papers.title: Editorial: Injuries, injury prevention and training in climbing - volume II.
papers.journal: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
papers.abstract: A collection of 2025 research highlighting that climbing injuries have shifted from acute trauma to overuse (chronic) patterns as the sport matures. Discusses the efficacy of new treatment algorithms for capsulitis and the impact of 'hangboard' training loads.
findings: New evidence suggests warm-ups with climbing-specific activities are more protective than general cross-training.
Adolescent climbers (15-19 years) show an 11.3x higher injury risk than younger climbers (11-14 years), marking a critical window for overuse intervention.
papers.implications: Implement strict volume monitoring for adolescent climbers (ages 15-19), as this is the peak window for overuse injury development.
papers.stats: 11.3x higher risk in late adolescence; Overuse > Acute prevalence.
